front: Film: Patricia (Jean Seberg) sells the New York Herald Tribune and betrays her lover Michel to the police; Michel is shot in the street
back: Breathless / À bout de souffle (Jean-Luc Godard)

front: Film: Michel Poiccard (Jean-Paul Belmondo) is obsessed with Humphrey Bogart and repeatedly rubs his lips in imitation
back: Breathless / À bout de souffle (Jean-Luc Godard)

front: Film: ends with the famously ambiguous word 'dégueulasse' ('disgusting'/'puke') — Patricia asks what it means as her lover Michel is dying
back: Breathless / À bout de souffle (Jean-Luc Godard)

front: Film: pioneered jump cuts — edits between shots that do not preserve continuity, notably in a scene of Jean Seberg in sunglasses in a convertible
back: Breathless / À bout de souffle (Jean-Luc Godard)

front: Film: Patricia poses in front of a Renoir portrait and asks whether she's prettier than it; in the final scene she imitates her dead lover's Bogart lip-rubbing gesture
back: Breathless / À bout de souffle (Jean-Luc Godard)

front: Film: soundtrack composed by Martial Solal
back: Breathless / À bout de souffle (Jean-Luc Godard)

front: Film: three main characters (Odile, Franz, Arthur) perform the 'Madison Dance' in a cafe — completely unrelated to the actual Madison dance; soundtrack goes silent for 36 seconds
back: Bande à part / Band of Outsiders (Jean-Luc Godard)

front: Film: the trio breaks the record for running through the Louvre (previously held by Jimmy Johnson of San Francisco); camera lingers on The Oath of the Horatii
back: Bande à part / Band of Outsiders (Jean-Luc Godard)

front: Film: inspired the name of Quentin Tarantino's production company A Band Apart Films; the Madison dance sequence inspired the disco scene in Pulp Fiction
back: Bande à part / Band of Outsiders (Jean-Luc Godard)

front: Film: title sequence shifts between three faces as white letters appear one by one on screen, beginning with an accented 'a'
back: Bande à part / Band of Outsiders (Jean-Luc Godard)

front: Film: protagonist paints his face blue and straps red and yellow dynamite to his head and blows himself up
Film: Sam Fuller appears describing cinema as 'a battleground — love, hate, action, violence, death — in one word: emotions'
Film: a pen changes a word meaning 'art' to a word meaning 'death' before the protagonist blows himself up
Film: long traveling shot along an epic traffic jam; ends with Corinne and Roland captured by hippie cannibals
Film: split into twelve chapters; follows Nana (Anna Karina), an aspiring actress who becomes a prostitute and dies under Raoul
back: Vivre sa Vie / My Life to Live (Jean-Luc Godard)
work: Vivre sa Vie (My Life to Live, 1962)

front: Film: uses a recurring slow two-bar organ theme; scene where Nana in a sailor suit performs a striptease directly into the camera
back: Vivre sa Vie / My Life to Live (Jean-Luc Godard)
work: Vivre sa Vie (My Life to Live, 1962)

front: Film: opens with a continuous four-minute take of Lemmy Caution taking an elevator to his hotel room; ends with Natacha von Braun telling him she loves him after he destroys a supercomputer
Film: long close-up of swirling coffee accompanied by a whispered monologue; narrator says 'when I speak, I limit the world'
back: Two or Three Things I Know About Her (Jean-Luc Godard)
work: Two or Three Things I Know About Her (1967)

front: Film: centers on communist Paul and pop singer Madeleine; intertitle reads 'This film could be called The Children of Marx and Coca-Cola'
Film: producers forced the director to include a nude scene with Brigitte Bardot; Fritz Lang plays himself; about making a film adaptation of The Odyssey
Film: copies of Mao's Little Red Book are thrown at a toy tank; features the song 'Mao Mao' by Claude Channes; a character expelled for liking Johnny Guitar
Director: frequent collaborator with Anna Karina, his then-wife, who starred in many of his 1960s films
Director: 1960s films feature extensive use of red, white, and blue intertitles
Director: became increasingly Marxist in later career; member of the Dziga Vertov Group
Director: died in Switzerland by assisted suicide in 2022
Director: long-time cinematographer was Raoul Coutard
Director: married Anne Wiazemsky a year after her appearance in Robert Bresson's Au Hasard Balthazar
Director: Isabelle Huppert starred in his 1980 film Every Man for Himself
